questions.prompt: Your prompt is producing mediocre output. What's the highest-leverage fix to try BEFORE upgrading to a larger model?

choices.text: Add 'You are a world-class expert' to the system prompt.

Make the prompt more specific (role, audience, tone, length, format) AND add 1–2 labeled examples of the expected output. Specificity + examples routinely match or exceed a model upgrade at a fraction of the cost.
Add temperature: 2.
Wrap everything in ALL CAPS.

questions.explanation: Specificity is the most underrated lever in prompt engineering. Vague prompts produce vague output regardless of model. One well-chosen labeled example often outperforms paragraphs of description. Try this before switching models — the cost difference is often 5–10× for marginal quality gain.

questions.prompt: Which OpenAI feature guarantees the model's response is parseable JSON conforming to a schema?

choices.text: Asking for JSON in the prompt.

Structured output with strict: true, an explicit `required` array, and additionalProperties: false. The model is constrained at token generation time and can't emit malformed JSON or skip required fields.
Temperature: 0.
A reasoning model.

questions.explanation: Structured output is the actual API feature. With strict mode + required + additionalProperties:false, structural conformance is enforced. Asking for JSON in a prompt is a soft request — the model usually complies but not always. Use the real feature.

questions.prompt: Your function call to fetch user data fails with a database error. What should you send back to the model?

choices.text: Crash your code with an exception.

Send the error back as a tool result (e.g., { error: 'Database unreachable' }). The model sees the error and can respond appropriately — apologize, retry, try a different tool, or ask the user for clarification. Tool errors are first-class data, not crashes.
Return fake successful data so the user doesn't notice.
Silently ignore the error and return nothing.

questions.explanation: Errors flow back as tool results, not as exceptions. The model handles them gracefully — it can apologize, retry, or try a different approach. This is what makes agents recoverable instead of fragile. Faking data is the worst option; it produces hallucinations.

questions.prompt: You're embedding a 10,000-word internal manual for semantic search. What's the right chunking strategy?

choices.text: Embed the whole document as a single vector.

Chunk into ~500–800-token passages (with some overlap), embed each chunk separately, store both the embedding and the chunk text. Whole-document embeddings smear meaning across topics and produce weak retrieval.
Embed each sentence individually.
Chunk into 50-token mini-passages.

questions.explanation: 500–800-token chunks are the typical sweet spot: large enough to carry meaningful context, small enough that each chunk represents one coherent idea. Whole-document embeddings average meaning across many topics; sentence-level chunks lose context. Always store both the vector AND the original text.

questions.prompt: You're building a phone-call-style AI agent. Users expect natural back-and-forth. Which OpenAI capability should you reach for?

choices.text: Sequential STT → GPT-5 → TTS calls — it's the simplest.

The Realtime API. Sequential calls introduce 2–4 seconds of latency per turn, which breaks the interactive feel. Realtime streams bidirectional audio with sub-second response latency including model processing.
Image generation.
Embeddings.

questions.explanation: For interactive voice where latency is the product, the Realtime API is the right tool — sub-second latency, bidirectional streaming, supports tools mid-conversation. Sequential STT → LLM → TTS works for any voice product but feels clunky for live conversation. Use Realtime when latency matters; sequential when it doesn't.
